items.title: Why osteoporosis drug offset patterns differ by class
items.text: Bisphosphonates bind bone for years with slow offset; denosumab offset is rapid with rebound; anabolics lose BMD gains unless followed by antiresorptives.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Anabolics (teriparatide, abaloparatide, romosozumab) Bone mineral density (BMD) gains during treatment are lost in the months following cessation. Therefore, for each of these agents, it is important to lock in the BMD gains with the use of an antiresorptive agent"

items.title: How long alendronate persists after stopping
items.text: After 5 years alendronate, a 1-year holiday is often appropriate; FLEX showed 5-year holiday does not increase nonvertebral fractures though vertebral fractures may rise.
items.reference: Case 2: "The FLEX study indicates that a 5-year drug holiday will not increase her risk of nonvertebral fractures, but there may be a rise in the incidence of clinical vertebral fractures."

items.title: How risedronate offset differs from alendronate
items.text: Risedronate turnover suppression offsets within 12 months with femoral neck BMD returning to baseline—faster than alendronate or zoledronate.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "Studies of risedronate demonstrate a more rapid offset of bone turnover suppression, within 12 months of drug discontinuation."

items.title: Why annual zoledronate is often unnecessary
items.text: A single 5-mg IV dose inhibits turnover >5 years with hip BMD still 2% above baseline—clinicians often extend dosing intervals to 2–4 years.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "its inhibition of bone turnover lasts for more than 5 years after a single dose, and hip BMD is still 2% above baseline at that time point."

items.title: How denosumab discontinuation causes catastrophic vertebral fractures
items.text: After long-term denosumab, 15% develop vertebral fractures within months of stopping and 10% have multiple fractures—rebound resorption rises 50% above pretreatment.
items.reference: Denosumab: "15% of women discontinuing the drug developed vertebral fractures in the following months, and in 10% of discontinuing participants, there were multiple vertebral fractures."

items.title: How to transition off denosumab safely
items.text: Continue denosumab or transition to bisphosphonate—never stop without bridging; oral alendronate after 1 year denosumab prevents bone loss; zoledronate is only partial protection after long-term denosumab.
items.reference: Denosumab: "After 1 year of denosumab treatment, transitioning to oral alendronate appears to provide good prevention of bone loss."

items.title: Why teriparatide must be followed by antiresorptive therapy
items.text: After 18–24 months teriparatide, hip BMD returns nearly to baseline within a year off drug—convention is immediate transition to bisphosphonate or denosumab.
items.reference: Case 3: "Bone loss commences once teriparatide is stopped, so neither a drug holiday (Answer B) nor permanent cessation of treatment (Answer C) is appropriate."

items.title: How romosozumab discontinuation behaves
items.text: Stopping romosozumab causes substantial first-year BMD loss and BTM rebound similar to denosumab—transition to denosumab or alendronate maintains gains.
items.reference: Anabolics: "Its discontinuation is followed by substantial BMD loss over the first year off treatment, and bone resorption markers show a rebound similar to that following denosumab."

items.title: How FLEX changed alendronate continuation strategy
items.text: After 5 years alendronate, half-dose (70 mg every 2 weeks) maintains vertebral fracture prevention without full-dose continuation.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "after 5 years treatment, the dosage of alendronate can be reduced by one-half without loss of efficacy."

items.title: Why offset must be discussed at treatment initiation
items.text: Patients starting nonbisphosphonate drugs—especially denosumab—must be warned not to discontinue without medical consultation, like beta-blockers in coronary disease.
items.reference: Anabolics: "the same warning must be provided to those starting nonbisphosphonate osteoporosis drugs, particularly denosumab."

items.title: How to manage 5-year denosumab patient wanting to stop
items.text: With hip T-score -2.2 and no fractures, either continue denosumab or transition to bisphosphonate—never unbridged cessation.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: A or D) Continuation of current therapy or transition to a bisphosphonate"

items.title: Why unplanned discontinuation is common and dangerous with denosumab
items.text: More than half of osteoporosis patients discontinue within 2 years—denosumab stoppers without bridging may fare worse than never having started.
items.reference: Denosumab: "more than half of patients treated for osteoporosis discontinue their medications within 2 years of initiation."

items.question: A woman on denosumab 5 years, fracture-free, hip T-score -2.2, wants to continue therapy. Best recommendation?
options: Continue denosumab or transition to bisphosphonate
items.explanation: Either continuation or bisphosphonate transition is acceptable; unbridged stopping risks rebound vertebral fractures.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: A or D) Continuation of current therapy or transition to a bisphosphonate"

items.question: Why is permanent denosumab cessation dangerous in Case 1?
options: Denosumab causes permanent BMD gain without maintenance
Rebound bone resorption can cause multiple vertebral fractures
Denosumab must be taken lifelong by FDA mandate
items.explanation: Rapid offset with rebound resorption causes vertebral fractures in up to 15% after long-term use.
items.reference: Denosumab: "15% of women discontinuing the drug developed vertebral fractures in the following months"

items.question: A woman on alendronate 5 years, fracture-free, hip T-score -2.2. Best recommendation?
items.explanation: 1- or 5-year drug holiday is preferred; FLEX supports holiday without increased nonvertebral fracture risk.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: B or C) A 1-year drug holiday or a 5-year drug holiday"

items.question: Alternative to holiday after 5 years alendronate per FLEX?
options: Reduce to half-dose (70 mg every 2 weeks) without loss of efficacy
items.explanation: FLEX showed half-dose alendronate maintains vertebral fracture prevention after 5 years full dose.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "after 5 years treatment, the dosage of alendronate can be reduced by one-half without loss of efficacy."

items.question: Why is permanent alendronate cessation likely inappropriate in Case 2?
options: If there was reasonable indication to start, permanent stop risks return of fracture risk
Hip T-score -2.2 mandates lifelong full-dose therapy
items.explanation: If initial treatment was justified, permanent cessation without reassessment is usually inappropriate.
items.reference: Case 2: "If there was a reasonable indication for the initiation of alendronate in the first place, then permanent cessation of treatment (Answer D) is likely to be inappropriate."

items.question: A woman completed 2 years teriparatide, fracture-free, hip T-score -2.2. Best next step?
items.explanation: Teriparatide is limited to 18–24 months; BMD loss follows cessation unless antiresorptive follow-on is given.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: D) Transition to a bisphosphonate or denosumab"

items.question: Why can teriparatide not be continued beyond 2 years?
options: Human osteosarcoma signal in rat studies limits duration to 18–24 months
It loses all effect after 6 months
FDA bans all anabolic agents after 1 year
BMD always decreases while on teriparatide
items.explanation: High-dose long-term teriparatide increased osteosarcoma in rats—clinical use capped at 18–24 months though human signal absent.
items.reference: Case 3: "the use of teriparatide is restricted to 18 to 24 months across various jurisdictions."

items.question: How quickly does risedronate's effect offset after stopping?
items.explanation: Risedronate turnover suppression offsets within 12 months—faster than alendronate.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "Studies of risedronate demonstrate a more rapid offset of bone turnover suppression, within 12 months of drug discontinuation."

items.question: After a single 5-mg zoledronate infusion, how long does turnover suppression last?
items.explanation: Zoledronate has highest bone affinity—single dose suppresses turnover >5 years.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "its inhibition of bone turnover lasts for more than 5 years after a single dose"

items.question: After missing one denosumab dose, bone resorption can rise to:
items.explanation: Phase 2 data show dramatic offset with resorption ~50% above pretreatment within months of missed dose.
items.reference: Denosumab: "bone resorption rising to about 50% above pretreatment levels within months of drug discontinuation."

items.question: After 1 year denosumab, which transition best prevents bone loss?
items.explanation: Transitioning to oral alendronate after 1 year denosumab provides good bone loss prevention.
items.reference: Denosumab: "After 1 year of denosumab treatment, transitioning to oral alendronate appears to provide good prevention of bone loss."

items.question: After completing 12 months romosozumab, necessary next step?
options: Antiresorptive therapy to maintain BMD gains
items.explanation: Substantial BMD loss follows romosozumab cessation unless denosumab or alendronate follow-on is given.
items.reference: Anabolics: "if patients transition to denosumab or to alendronate, positive changes in BMD are observed and fracture rates remain low."

items.question: When should drug offset characteristics be discussed with osteoporosis patients?
options: At treatment initiation — especially for denosumab
Only after first fracture on therapy
items.explanation: Offset counseling at initiation allows informed therapeutic strategy—critical for denosumab.
items.reference: Anabolics: "it is critically important to counsel patients about the offset characteristics of osteoporosis drugs when they are first being prescribed"

items.statement: A 1-year drug holiday after 5 years of alendronate is often appropriate for risedronate and 12–24 months for alendronate per main conclusions.
items.explanation: Suggested discontinuation: 6 months holiday for risedronate, 12–24 months for alendronate when holiday is indicated.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "If a drug holiday is judged to be indicated, 6 months is usually appropriate for risedronate and 12 to 24 months for alendronate."

items.statement: A drug holiday is appropriate after long-term denosumab if BMD has improved.
items.explanation: Denosumab requires continuous therapy or bridged transition—no drug holiday.
items.reference: Case 1: "Cessation of therapy, whether temporary or permanent (Answers B and C), would expose her to risk of rebound vertebral fractures."

items.statement: Bone density gains persist indefinitely after stopping teriparatide without follow-on therapy.
items.explanation: Hip BMD returns nearly to baseline within a year; antiresorptive follow-on is required.
items.reference: Anabolics: "One year after conclusion of a 1-year treatment course, spine BMD is still several percent above baseline, but hip BMD is only about 0.5% above baseline."

items.statement: Annual zoledronate is necessary in most patients because effects last only 6 months.
items.explanation: Single dose suppresses turnover >5 years—annual dosing often unnecessary.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Zoledronate inhibits bone resorption for more than 5 years, so annual dosing is unnecessary in most circumstances."

items.statement: Up to 15% of patients stopping long-term denosumab experience vertebral fractures.
items.explanation: FREEDOM extension documented this rebound fracture risk.
items.reference: Denosumab: "15% of women discontinuing the drug developed vertebral fractures in the following months"

items.statement: FLEX showed continuing alendronate after 5 years still reduces clinical vertebral fractures by about half.
items.explanation: Continued therapy reduced clinical vertebral fractures ~50% vs placebo in FLEX.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "clinical vertebral fractures were reduced by about one-half in those who continued to take alendronate."

items.statement: Romosozumab discontinuation causes BTM rebound similar to denosumab.
items.explanation: Resorption markers rebound after romosozumab cessation unless antiresorptive follow-on given.
items.reference: Anabolics: "bone resorption markers show a rebound similar to that following denosumab."

items.statement: After long-term denosumab, zoledronate provides complete protection against bone loss.
items.explanation: Zoledronate provides only partial protection after prolonged denosumab.
items.reference: Denosumab: "after long-term denosumab treatment, zoledronate is only partially effective in preventing subsequent bone loss."

items.assertion: Assertion: Denosumab must not be stopped without transition to another antiresorptive.
items.reason: Reason: Bone resorption rises to 50% above pretreatment after missed dose.
items.explanation: Both true—rapid antibody clearance drives rebound resorption and vertebral fracture risk.
items.reference: Denosumab: "bone resorption rising to about 50% above pretreatment levels within months of drug discontinuation."

items.assertion: Assertion: A 5-year alendronate holiday does not increase nonvertebral fracture risk per FLEX.
items.reason: Reason: Bisphosphonates bind bone mineral for years after stopping.
items.explanation: Both true but R explains persistence mechanism rather than specifically the nonvertebral fracture finding.
items.reference: Case 2: "a 5-year drug holiday will not increase her risk of nonvertebral fractures"

items.assertion: Assertion: Teriparatide should be followed by bisphosphonate or denosumab.
items.reason: Reason: BMD gains are lost within months after anabolic cessation.
items.explanation: Both true—anabolic benefit must be locked in with antiresorptive follow-on.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "it is important to lock in the BMD gains with the use of an antiresorptive agent (bisphosphonate or denosumab) at the time of anabolic withdrawal."

items.assertion: Assertion: Annual zoledronate is unnecessary in most patients.
items.reason: Reason: A single 5-mg dose suppresses turnover for more than 5 years.
items.explanation: Both true—prolonged skeletal retention obviates yearly dosing in many patients.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Zoledronate inhibits bone resorption for more than 5 years, so annual dosing is unnecessary in most circumstances."

items.assertion: Assertion: Risedronate offset occurs faster than alendronate.
items.reason: Reason: Risedronate has lower affinity for bone mineral than zoledronate.
items.explanation: Both true but affinity comparison to zoledronate rather than alendronate is the stated mechanism in text.
items.reference: Bisphosphonates: "Thus, zoledronate has the longest duration of action, whereas the effects of risedronate and ibandronate are more transient."

items.assertion: Assertion: Continuing denosumab is acceptable for a 5-year fracture-free patient with hip T-score -2.2.
items.reason: Reason: Hip T-score above -2.5 always mandates stopping therapy.
items.explanation: A is acceptable per Case 1; R is false—T-score alone does not mandate stopping.
items.reference: Case 1: "Either continuing current therapy (Answer A) or transitioning to a bisphosphonate (Answer D) would be acceptable answers."

items.assertion: Assertion: Romosozumab cessation requires antiresorptive follow-on.
items.reason: Reason: Romosozumab causes substantial BMD loss in the first year off treatment.
items.explanation: Both true—rebound loss mirrors denosumab unless bridged to antiresorptive.
items.reference: Anabolics: "Its discontinuation is followed by substantial BMD loss over the first year off treatment"

items.assertion: Assertion: Patients starting denosumab should be counseled about offset before discontinuing.
items.reason: Reason: More than half of osteoporosis patients discontinue therapy within 2 years.
items.explanation: Both true—high real-world discontinuation rates make upfront offset counseling essential.
items.reference: Denosumab: "more than half of patients treated for osteoporosis discontinue their medications within 2 years of initiation."
